title: Chapter 6 - HTTPS and APIs
description: Check if you understand HTTPS, APIs, and common API styles.

questions.prompt: What is the main advantage of HTTPS over HTTP?

options.label: It removes the need for servers
It encrypts and protects the connection using TLS

explanations.overview: HTTPS uses TLS to encrypt traffic and protect it from tampering, and it helps verify server identity with certificates.

byOptionId.b: HTTPS does not change URL length in a useful way.
byOptionId.d: Correct. TLS adds encryption and integrity protections.

questions.prompt: On the web, what is an API most commonly used for?

options.label: To let different programs request data or actions from a server
To store your CSS in a special folder

explanations.overview: APIs help software talk to other software, often using HTTP and JSON.

byOptionId.c: CSS storage is not what an API is for.
byOptionId.a: Correct. APIs expose data and actions to clients.
byOptionId.d: DNS is about name-to-IP lookup, not application data.

questions.prompt: Which option is a good example of a real-time, two-way API connection?

explanations.overview: WebSockets keep a connection open, letting both sides send messages any time.

byOptionId.c: REST usually uses separate request/response calls.
byOptionId.a: Correct. WebSockets are designed for real-time two-way messaging.
byOptionId.d: DNS isn’t an API style for app data; it’s a naming system.
byOptionId.b: HTML is a markup language, not an API.

questions.prompt: Which HTTP header indicates the response is JSON?

explanations.overview: APIs use the Content-Type header to tell clients the media type of the body.

byOptionId.c: `Accept` is sent by clients to indicate what they can accept.
byOptionId.b: `Location` is used for redirects or created resources, not media type.
byOptionId.d: `Server` advertises server software, not the body format.
